SHOBANA RADHAKRISHNAN:
Hi, I'm Shobana,

senior director of
engineering for TV at Google.

Today we'll dive into
the latest updates

from Google TV and our
underlying Android TV OS

and explore the ways you
can build excellent TV apps.

Last fall, we mentioned
that Google TV and Android

TV achieved over 270 million
monthly active devices,

establishing one of the
largest smart TV OS footprints.

We look forward to
increasing this momentum

by bringing our platform
to more form factors

and delivering more delightful
user experiences this year.

We are glad to see users
interact with our platform

across a diverse range of
entry-level to premium devices,

from TVs, phones, and tablets to
projectors and, most recently,
smart monitors.

We're especially proud to
see support for Google TV

on the awarded OMEN 32x smart
gaming monitor and have our new

Google TV Streamer recognized as
the best media streaming device

by the "New York
Times Wirecutter."

Today, we are excited
to share even more ways

users can access effortless
entertainment on Google
TV this year.

First off, we'll be
partnering with select OEMs

to launch affordable and
reliable low-RAM panel

TVs, allowing more
budget-conscious users

to experience quality
entertainment.

We are also excited to soon
bring Google TV to headsets,
powered by Android XR.

Users can see, hear,
and explore the action
from the best seat in the house.

They can access their
favorite content

across their subscriptions,
search for content,
and get curated recommendations.

Our broad platform availability
across a variety of form factors

directly translates to
more opportunities for you
to connect with users worldwide.

To further enrich
the user experience,

we brought more apps
to our global platform.

Just to name a few,
we have launched

HISTORY Vault in the US,
BBC, Channel 4, ARD, TF1,

and ITV in EMEA, as well as
JioHotstar and Vidio in the APAC
region.

Now I can watch my
favorite Bollywood movies
from these apps on Google TV.

Our quality streaming
partnerships

also allow us to sustain
strong app engagement.

In 2024, US users spent
approximately 200 minutes
in apps per day.

As our platform becomes
even more diversified

with excellent apps, we're
also making improvements

to help users find what to watch
and offer new ways for your apps
to reach your target users.

Last year, we previewed
the Video Discovery API
that's available for partners.

Video discovery optimizes
resumption, entitlements,

and recommendations across
all Google TV form factors

to enhance the end
user experience
and boost app engagement.

You can now easily
display user's post video

within the Continue Watching
row from the home screen.

Resumption drives 60% of all
user interactions on Google TV.

This interface allows
users to resume viewing

from where they left
off across any device

when they are signed in
with their Google account.

The Video Discovery
API also streamlines

entitlement management, which
matches content to eligibility.

This eligibility might be
from a subscription, purchase,
or a free ad-supported model.

Video discovery allows users
to share their entitlements

through one simple
opt-in process.

Users appreciate this because
they can enjoy personalized

recommendations without
needing to manually update
all their subscription details.

This allows you,
as our partners,

to connect with users across
multiple discovery points
on Google TV.

That means more immediate access
and increased app engagement.

The Video Discovery API even
highlights personalized content

recommendations based on content
that users watch inside apps.

Partners can begin incorporating
the Video Discovery API

today, starting with resumption
and entitlement integrations.

Check out the link for
more details to enroll.

Google TV is committed
to responsibly using AI

to bring more helpfulness
into the home too.

For instance, last fall, we
released AI-generated content

descriptions which help users
find their next favorite film
or series to watch.

Users can access bite-sized
season summaries,

learn more about a movie, and
see feedback from other viewers.

Google TV also offers
custom AI screensavers.

So users can simply
use their voice

to create stunning
screensavers for the largest
screen in their home.

Earlier this year, we announced
that we'll bring Gemini
capabilities to Google TV.

So users can speak
more naturally

and conversationally to find
what to watch and get answers
to complex questions.

Our new AI assistant
now understands

users' natural,
conversational voice searches.

It then analyzes titles from
your apps and shares tailored

recommendations, effectively
boosting the visibility
of your app's content.

Let's dive deeper into
what this looks like.

For instance, I want to share
my interest in the classic

"Jurassic Park" movies
with my younger kids,

but I don't want to show
them something too intense.

Now I can easily ask questions
like, I like Jurassic park.

What are some movies like it
that I can watch with my kids?

And voila, I get
personalized recommendations,

perfect for my
next family night.

Plus, users can
naturally ask questions

to explore their
latest curiosities

and get insightful answers
with supporting videos
for deeper learning.

They can invite the whole family
to learn, asking questions like,

can you explain the solar
system to my first grader?

And the AI assistant will
provide a personalized response

with suitable video
suggestions that a first grader
would be excited to see.

When we demoed Google
TV with Gemini at CES,

we were pleased to receive the
best AI award from Tom's Guide.

We are so excited to bring this
new, helpful, and delightful
experience to users this fall.

There's never been a better
time to be a Google TV
partner or developer.

Over to you, Paul.

We've seen three
common challenges

when handling focus

first, setting initial focus.

Second, taking over control

And third, restoring focus.

Let's explore setting
initial focus.

In a TV UI, whenever

the initial focus
needs to be set.

Compose for TV does this
in the majority of cases,

but sometimes the default might
differ from your preference.

You can control that initial
focus in three steps.

First, declare a
focus requester that's
wrapped in a member function.

Then apply a focus requester
modifier on the first card.

And finally, call the
request focus method

in the launched effects
composable function.

This last bit ensures
that the focus

is moved to the right element
when the composable is
composed for the first time.

Now let's take a look
at some scenarios

where focus moves to an
unexpected component.

Here, we have four items
arranged in rows and columns.

When I press the
right D-pad button,

the focus moves from the
first item to the third item.
That's odd.

To understand why
this is happening,

we can look at our
layout hierarchy.

The four focus targets
are at the same level,

and ThirdCard is the
first item to the right
of FirstCard in the layout.

That's why focus
moves from the first

to the third with the
right directional key.

We can resolve this
by creating a focus

group that consists of the
focus targets inside the row.

Now, since the row
and the first card

are the only focus
targets at the same level,

pressing the right
directional key no longer

moves to the third card
from within a focus group.

We should also make sure
that focus is restored
when the user returns to it.

By using the focus
restore modifier,

we can make sure
that the focus is

brought to the right
element in the group.

Now, these are

Our goal is to make

We're excited to announce the
upcoming release of Android 16
for TV.

Developers can begin using
the latest beta today.

Android 16 includes some great
features to help TV developers.

First, the new media
quality API provides

a standardized interface
to developers and vendors
to manage picture profiles.

For movies and TV series that
are mastered with a wider

dynamic range, developers
might request filmmaker mode

to accurately display content
as the creator intended
for it to look.

A cinema profile with
greater color accuracy

brings out subtle
details and shadows

in favor of
increasing brightness.

Conversely, live
sporting events,

which are often mastered
with a narrow dynamic range

and watched in
daylight, can benefit

from a profile that gives
preference to brightness
over color accuracy.

If you'd like to understand
which profile a device running

Android 16 or later supports,
query MediaQuality manager using
getAvailablePictureProfiles.

The profile ID may match against
a known system profile, some
of which may differ by OEM.

To apply a specific profile, you
can query MediaQuality manager
by profile ID--

for example, for this
one for cinema profile.

You can then apply
this picture profile

as a parameter to mediaCodec
by providing in a parcelable
into its bundle.

Selecting a picture
profile in ExoPlayer

will be available in a future
release later this year.

You can find the list
of predefined profiles

in the updated guide for
adjusting display settings.

Note that these
presets aren't fixed.

TVs come with some
preset profiles,

but OEMs can include more,
and users can update them
based on their preferences.

Another new feature
in Android 16

is platform support
for Eclipsa audio.

This is a new opensource
spatial audio codec

based on the immersive audio
model and formats developed

by Google, Samsung, and
other key contributors

within the Alliance
for Open Media.

It's designed to revolutionize
immersive audio experiences

across a wide range
of applications,

including streaming and gaming
in augmented or virtual reality.

And because the
format is open source,

anyone can use
freely-available audio tools

or leverage automated
workflows that use FFmpeg
to create Eclipsa audio files.

You can already begin using
these codecs today, even

on older platforms, by using
the built-in support in Media3
ExoPlayer from version 1.5.

By compiling the
Lib IAMF library,

you'll get a dedicated
audio renderer instance.

For details on
how to compile it,

see the decoder extension
guide on GitHub.

Android 16 for TV now
supports 64-bit kernels,

which means you can run the
emulator natively on a 64-bit
host machine.

Although there aren't any
64-bit Android TV devices yet,

it shows how we're laying
the engineering framework.

We recommend that you also

And remember that you
